turns.name: REHNOUIST
turns.text: The next case is No. 86-1042, City of Lakewood versus Plain Dealer Publishing Company. Whenever you are ready, Mr. Fischer. ORAL ARGUMENT BY HENRY B. FISCHER, ESQUIRE

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, this case involves an ordinance of the City of Lakewood that gives the mayor contract authority to rent the city's treeline property for the placement of news boxese There are three issues that I would like to relate before the Court today. The three are jurisdiction, discretion, and insurance. Whether there is jurisdiction to pass on the merits of the case, which was pointed out at Page 43 of the brief, whether the discretion given by the ordinance to the mayor and the architect review board are so uncontrolled as to be unconstitutional, and the constitutionality of the insurance requirement. We will attempt to demonstrate the Plain Dealer did not apply for a permit, did not exhaust its administrative remedies and appeals, and did not properly test the ordinance to assert the issues before the Court to permit passing on the merits of the ordinance. We will try to demonstrate that the discretion is not uncontrolled, and that the insurance requirement, being content neutral and otherwise reasonable, is constitutional. Factually, the original Lakewood ordinance banned all structures, not just news boxes, all structures on the city property. Lakewood did not appeal. They wanted to permit the Plain Dealer to put their news boxes in. And they amended their ordinances twice to accommodate the Plain Dealer's placement of news boxes along the streets. The record shows that had the Plain Dealer applied, they-would have secured the spaces they desired. The city engineer so testified, and the court also found so. There would have been no discretion of the mayor to have been applied and they could have had the news boxes installed since 1984.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: There was no discretion?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: There wouldn't have been any discretion for the mayor to exercise if they had applied, because there was nothing wrong with where they wanted to place their news boxes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well , what was the authority given to the mayor by the ordinance?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: To rent the property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, but it was subject to his control, wasn't it?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: It was subject to his determination. It is right in the ordinance. If the location was not causing a health or safety hazard — it is written right in the ordinance. If it is not a health or safety hazard. Also, if it is not causing a nuisance or not unduly interfering with the.rights of the traffic, that the location would be all right. Those were -the standards that were set up —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And you think then if those were satisfied the mayor had to issue the permit?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That's right. He was prepared to issue the permit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that what the court below found?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, starting with Finding 16.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did he say? What did it say?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: It said that the city was ready, willing, and able to issue the permits had they applied but the Plain Dealer had not applied for the permits.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What did it say about the breadth of the ordinance? What was the court's below view of the mayor's discretion?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Well, the District Court found the ordinance to be constitutional all the way down the line.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: So they found — the District Court obviously found that as to everything before it the mayor did not have unbridled discretion, that the discretion was

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That the mayor did not have — they and did not find anything wrong with it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But there was the provision, wasn't there, that the mayor might deny or grant the application imposing conditions deemed necessary and reasonable?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That's correct, that he would not — he could not just deny it. If he denied it, he had to state his reasons, and there could only be three reasons that he could deny it. One would be location, that there was a health and safety hazard caused by it. The second reason that he could deny would be because it was not —- they could not harmonize it through architectural review.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Is that the limit of necessary and reasonable?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Necessary and reasonable? This, we are talking about contract authority, and just like many Other contracts that have come before this Court, the legislative authority gives an individual authority to make contracts within certain guidlines. It gives them discretion for the nuts and bolts details. The nuts and bolts detail some repairs on the sidewalk or whatever? They would want to make some arrangements for the Plain Dealer to move their news box during that particular time. So that is the necessary and reasonable type of regulations we are talking about. .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the Court of Appeals thought :.that the mayor was given too broad discretion.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That's correct, and our position is that that is one of the things we are here to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So we have to disagree with them as to the meaning of the ordinance?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: If you get to that beyond the jurisdiction, because we start with jurisdiction, and our contention is and has been, because of the appeal procedure that we have established, they should have applied for their permit first, and if they didn't like the conditions, which I submit to you I don't see any reason why they wouldn't have 1iked- them, I think they would have had their news boxes out there since 1984, there would have been no problem.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer, did you make a jurisdictional argument in your jurisdictional statement, in your brief on the merits?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, you can find it at Page 31 §f our brief and essentially we cited the case of Greer versus Spock, that they should have — that the thing was not ripe for testing. We also asserted a defense in the District Court that they had this administrative remedy and. that they should have asserted it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Page 31 of the blue brief?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes. The brief on the merits. Is it 43? Excuse me, it is 43. I'm sorry, Page 43 in our brief.' ■The ordinance was written, I can tell you from firsthand because I wrote it, the ordinance was written so that the City of Lakewood could permit them to place their news boxes without interfering with the constitutional rights of other persons, e.g., Lovell, Schneider, other people that would want to use that property but couldn't, and the reason that they couldn't was because the Plain Dealer wanted to use it exclusively. We used the rental theory to overcome that. Yes?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I missed that page.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: It is Page 43.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Forty-three.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible.) Is that it? MR., FISCHER: Pardon? WUESTION: The discussion of the Gannett satellite case, is that what you are referring to on Page 43?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Gannett and also we led up to that with Greer versus Spock on Page 41 — or 42.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: So PD did not apply. Instead, they sued to attack the ordinances instead, and I think the essence is that they want free, uninhabited use of the city property. That is where they are coming from. So, as to jurisdiction, as I indicated, the city had drafted this ordinance with the attempt to permit, not to play games with the Plain Dealer. We wrote this ordinance so that the Plain Dealer could place their news boxes, and we felt the appropriate theory for allowing this was a rental theory. And you could find in the finding 16 of the Court and I think it is Page A30 of the jiirisdictional statements where that finding is, that they would have been able to get these news box locations, but instead they choose to attack the whole thing. The District Court would not receive any evidence of administrative interpretation. We offered the testimony of the mayor who actually requested the legislation to settle the lawsuit. The District Court, however, would not allow us to use that testimony as to what was intended. There are no applications. There is no real justiciable issues. It is not ripe because they did not apply it. Now, as to the standards — up some kind of unlimited discretion in the mayor to grant or deny the permit, and if the court below was correct about that, then I assume jurisdiction would lie, wouldn't it, to make a facial challenge.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I believe — I hate to compare cases, but I believe that we have set out far more guidelines than the Gannett case or the Greer versus Spock case. We have limited discretion tremendously compared to those. I don't think the statement could even be made. I hope I have answered your question on that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are just disagreeing with the District Court.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are just disagreeing with the courts below.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I am disagreeing with the Court of Appeals.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On the construction of the ordinance.

turns.name: FSICHER
turns.text: Right, not the District Court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: We have the provisions right in the ordinance as to, you can't make it any different height, you can't make it any different size, you can't put it next to a fire plug. All these things are spelled out. The only thing that is left for the mayor to do is to check to see if architectural review was done, to be sure that it is a safe location, and to put any other conditions — for example, the condition to move the box in the case they have to do construction*. That is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We do have to disagree with the con­ struction of the ordinance with the Court of Appeals? MR* FISCHER: Yes, we do. We also believe that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: On your jurisdictional arqument.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Under jurisdiction I don't believe

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer, may I go back? You cited us to the findings on Page A30. You said they said they would grant the permission. As I read that, it only said they would grant them in the commercial districts, but not along Clifton Boulevard at the sites requested, all of which lie in the residential district.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That's correct, and the Court of Appeals sustained the City of Lakewood on that part ofthe ordinance that they could limit them to the commercial districts only. They also sustained that the rental charge could be made.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So the residential issue is not here.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Mo, the residential issue is not before this Court. As to discretion, the ordinance specified —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let me — excuse me.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I know you —

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You keep trying to go along. I am sorry. You say there was nothing left for the mayor to do, but .there is sort of a residual clause in the ordinance which says he can place, you know, whatever other conditions are reasonable, and there is no limitation on'what that might

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I think that these are terms of art. I believe probably hundreds of contracts have come before this Court where the legislative body gives authority to somebody to make a contract, they don't spell out every last term, and as long as the intent of the ordinance, which is to allow the placement of the news boxes is affected, I

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they don't all deal with the First Amendment, either. I mean, that is what our case law says, that you have to be particularly careful and particular­ ly specific when you are affecting First Amendment rights. That is just a pretty broad — . . MR, FISCHER: That's correct, and it could be only technical, minor things that would be reasonable and necessary, such as the relocation of the box in the event of construction, as the example that I have cited.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do I know that?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: How do you "know that?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How do T know that those are the only things that come within that language? What is the precise language again? I am looking for the ordinance here.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Such other conditions — it is C7, such other conditions as may be deemed reasonable and necessary by the mayor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Such other conditions as may be deemed reasonable and necessary by the mayor.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn't say technical conditions. That could mean anything in the world, couldn't it?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I don't believe that it does. I think the intent of the legislature was clear that they wanted the news box rental to go on, and it could be only the catch- all type clauses that you would throw into any contract to effectuate it, so that the city wouldn't be hamstrung. For example, with utility polls we have a right to ask them to move it and replace it during construction, and it was anticipated at the time that it was written that it was not going to contain every last nut and bolt, that there was going to be something for the mayor. We knew the Plain Dealer was going to apply, or thought they would apnly, at least, and we knew that we could handle those sites. We didn't know what else was going to come down the line, and so we left it open so that the mayor could protect the health and safety. It is a police power residual clause, just to protect the property rights of the city.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And there are no standards.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Pardon?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The board hasn't adopted any standards yet, have they?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Are you talking about the architectural review board?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I don't know whether they-have adopted them how*. This was shortly after the adoption —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they hadn't at the time it was filed.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They had not at the time it was filed.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They admitted they didn't.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That's right, because there had been nothing before them to work on so they had not proceeded to that point. The ordinance specifies size,, height,'location, eliminates considerable discretion. The most efficient procedure is right here as to what we have. If the mayor feels uncomfortable giving something that somebody asks they appeal to the council. The council has a legislative body that gives the contract authority wants to approve it that is the end of it. If they don't like what the council does with it, they go to the courts. And it is clear Ohio law that under Chapter 2506, the decision of that council cannot be arbitrary, it cannot be capricious, it cannot be illegal, it cannot be unconstitutional, and it cannot be unreasonable or unsupported by evidence. The mayor has to say why he does this or that or the other thing, and he has to give his reasons. I don't see what else we could do to make this any better, and certainly it is ten times — it is 100 times better than what you had in Greer versus Spock and also in the Gannett case. As to the mayor, we have the reasonable and necessary provision for relocation, as I indicated, and other things of that nature. The reasonable — we have the location standard provided, whether the architecture review board was done, the terms of the lease, and then plus the mayor must say if he is going to deny what the reasons .are, and it has to be only for the reasons of a health and safety hazard, or there is a nuisance, or that they would interfere with - the traffic. A lot has been said about censorship in this thing. one year term what is the mayor going to do, wait until there is an article in the newspaper and come up with some phony reason to cancel it? I doubt it. And then when they reapply for the next year, assuming that it is profitable to them and they want to continue with it, how would the mayor be able to come up with a good reason for denying when it was there the whole time? Either he has got a good reason for denying it or he doesn't. So I find it very difficult as to the censorship and I would point out that they would have had their permits if they had applied for them. It is clear on this reasonable and necessary and also these locational things that it is difficult to convene a council every time some new point comes up. That is why the mayor was given discretions and I submit that this contract authority with guidelines plus discretion is very typical for Congress, for state legislatures, for city councils all As to the architecture review board —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Excuse me. Suppose the mayor says one of the conditions I am attaching to this particular one is that it can only be usable by the public from 10:00 p.m. to midnight?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Then the person — if he would put that on there, then I believe the person would have the right to go to the council, and by the way, under 901.1(a) he can go directly to the council.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How can you go to the council? As I read this there is an appeal to the council if he refuses to grant or revokes a permit, but here he is granting it -subject to this conditon'. Does that — MR. FISCHER; That's right. We are not talking about a rigid thing. We have — a negotiated contract is what it is. If they insist on a contract without that provision they want it 24 hours a day.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Then he has to deny it.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: He would have to deny it. Then it would go to the council.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see. MR. FISCHER; However, I don't think that would even come up at all. As to architectural review, we are talking about the accepted and recognized architectural principles. They do have the power to make specific rules and expand their workbook so that there's more specifics. They never reached us at the time of trial because there was nothing before them, and here it is 1987, almost 1988. The Plain Dealer still hasn't applied. We are talking about properties. This has been all commercial strips and the big shopping centers. If you had telling the store front owner, you have to fix your place up, your signs have to be reduced in size, and so forth, so we end the blight. The owner pays for that sidewalk in front of the store, and he has to repair it if it is broke. He pays tax to the city also. Here we want to put the news box there. The only thing the city wants to do is to harmonize that design and color with what the people have in the storefront. I think it is only fair and reasonable that this be done. That is the purpose of it, to harmonize the installation. Otherwise, what is the use of fighting ail this blight and poor signage that is causing this blight? it will just end up that everybody is going to move to the shopping center,, and you can just forget about the fight to save the commercial districts. As to the architecture review, we do have the book of standards, and there are, I submit that the Court has probably not had a chance to look at that, because it defies placement in the appendix, but there are several pages showing the architecture of Lakewood and what would fit in. I don't think that would be any particular problem. I think it would have been extended without any trouble. There are standards. They are not going to be able to say we don't like the article you wrote about our book. Therefore architecturally we are not going to approve anything for you. It just wouldn't happen. As to insurance, the Plain Dealer has stated they never had an accident wtih any of these boxes. I just find it hard to believe that none of these boxes ever come back dinged up. I would imagine some of them have.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer, does the city require the furnishing of liability insurance and holding harmless for other similar devices that are placed on the streets, or is it just applicable to these news vending boxes?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: There are no other comparable devices placed on the street.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What about telephone booths?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: The telephone booths —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — and all the rest? MR.. FISCHER: The telephone, booths are not the telephone booths of the telephone company. These are telephones such as the Supreme Court has right here in the court. The city wants them there. They are a replacement for those little red boxes that you used to have that if you push the button or open the door the only thing that would be reported is the location of something happening. They put these telephone booths, or not booths, they are actually just posts with telephones on them in the right-of-way as part of the emergency communication. That wav for free a person aan go and make a telephone call to the police department, to the fire department. They don't have to go to somebody's house if they need a tow truck or an ambulance. They don't have to call the police for that if they have their own private —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The city doesn't have any of these ordinary pay telephone booths?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: This is a pay telephone booth. I am not sure if all of them are like it, but the ones that the city has, you just dial 0 — in fact now I guess it is 911 in Cuyahoga County, and you get the emergency service. So there is no payment.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are those the only numbers you can call?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: For free. You can call -- if you wanted to call your tow truck, you would have to place your quarter or whatever in there to make that call.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, or if you wanted to call your wife and say, I'm going to be late for dinner.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Excactly. You would have to place coins in it for that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In any event, no insurance or hold harmless is required from the phone company?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: No, because these are the city's. This is rented or owned by the city. It is telephone equipment of the city.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about the bus shelters?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: The bus shelters, I would have to categorize that as the city's property also. They have bus stops all over the city, and where there's not what they call an RTA, Regional Transit Authority shelter, the city has benches that they have installed and pay for themselves. Now, what they have —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, is your transportation company public or private?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: It is public, and the people of Lakewood pay sales tax to subsidize the running of that bus service.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Owned by whom? You say it is public. The greater Cleveland area or what?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, it is the Cuyahoga County Regional Transit Authority. Everybody is taxed for that. Instead of Lakewood paying for the benches and having — we have the people maintain either one, but instead of them paying for the installation of those benches, RTA puts them in for free. That's the convenience for the residents. Everything else is on the utility polls. I don't believe —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) telephone polls? What about the telephone polls?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: There is no —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, a car can run into a telephone poll.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: There is just one set of polls. Those polls that you see in the photographs are CEI.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: CEI owns them?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Electric Illuminating Company poll, and everything is on those polls. The wires that carry electricity for the street lights, the wires that carry electricity for the traffic lights, the telephone wires, they are all on there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And CEI is a private company? 4 to regulation under — I guess it is called PUCO.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. But they don't have to — you don't require them to ensure against liability from a poll falling down or — have to make repairs and relocate their —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But they don't have to insure the city against suits to the city from the negligent placement of the polls or something like that?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: No, these easements were granted a long time ago, and they are also there for the purpose of providing the city service, which is the telephone communica­ tion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the wires on the polls, you have cable companies that have wires on those polls?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: The cable company by other Acts are permitted to have wires on those polls also.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And those wires could come down and hurt somebody, but does the cable company have to ensure the municipality against —

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: The cable television company does have to ensure. There is — this is not in the record, I would point out.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am just curious. What about the telephone company. They have wires up there. Do they have to insure?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: We do not have a requirement on them for insurance. And that is because they are providing the service to the city for the emergency communications network. They provide the other wires incidentally —

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They were temporarily suspended, and temporary measures put into place. We got a call ffrom the Plain Dealer"that said, we want to have those boxes, so we passed the ordinance. We are still waiting for their application.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I see.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: But that ordinance will dissolve within, a short time after the decision.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: By its own terras?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, the council either — if the council takes other measures, depending on the decision in this case, or 1 think it is a 60-day period, it is dissolved. That was to preserve our position in this appeal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer, I noticed some of.' the plaintiff's exhibits have in the. photographs USA Today units. Did the Gannett people get permits?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Ho.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And why — are they parties to the litigation?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They are not. The New York Times trier to join the suit but they were kicked out on an abstention grounds by the Sixth Circuit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You are not objecting to USA units?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They have not applied. We don't have anything to do with them. These USA Today units are in somebody else's city. They are not in the City of Lakewood.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So that Exhibit 41 is not in the City of Lakewood?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Let me check.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or 40?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: No, that is plaintiff's exhibit, and they put in a whole bunch of exhibits over our objection as to news boxes in other cities.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In the city?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: In other cities. ' We felt it was irrelevant, but the court let it in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So those boxes in those exhibits are not in your city.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: No. There are boxes in our city.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, yes.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: I think there were 16 sites where coffee shops, supermarkets. They are all over the city, within a quarter-mile —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are there any USA Today boxes in the city?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes. Yes, there are.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And what about them? Did they apply for a permit?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They have not seen fit to apply for a permit.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, why don't you throw them out?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Well, they are on private property.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, private property.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Either on private property or public property that is not owned by the City of Lakewood, so we have no jurisdiction. We are not acting as owner as to those. It would just be if a zoning would come up that we would apply apply to that..

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask on the — I aather probably the Plain Dealer has some on private property, too, don't they, or do they?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: They have private property?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They have these boxes on private property located within the city?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, they have 16.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are those boxes subjected to any kind of architectural design, zoning requirements?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: No. No, we have not subjected them to any of the requirements. I haven't looked at them as to — I don't think that it would apply to them since — because of where they are located, but it may be that we slipped up on that. I don't know. I don't think that — if we have, I think that it's because of their location it would not apply.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer, what about the devices shown, for example, on Plaintiff's Exhibit 34? What is that box?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: That is the junction box for the telephone company.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it is owned by a private company, the telehpone company?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Yes, it is, and it —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Do they have to provide insurance?

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: . No, they don't. That is there for carrying the lines not only of private but also of the city's communication system. This is part of the tradeoff for the consideration — actually, the city is getting the service on a lease basis. They may even make some money on some of these phones if people put coins in them. So this is part of the tradeoff that they would permit them because they are carrying emergency communications. There have been no rentals, no rentals of any city property that do not have insurance. As a matter of risk management, every contract that the City of Lakewood makes, unless they slip up, their intention is to have indemnifica­ tion and insurance. Otherwise —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible.)

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Any contract.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Any contract.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Any contract, whether it is —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But not when you grant an easement.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: An easement is an interest in real estate, which is done by a deed easement, and these things were done — they had lighting and electricity probably before the days of the Depression, so there is nothing we can do about it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: It is there. As to alternate — one other point here. The use of the sidewalk and tree lawn area has been exclusively used for the provision of traditional city services and utilities. There has been no structure erected there that does not provide the city with a service, and there is not such a thing there now. The traditional city services that are provided, newspaper and the sale of newspaper are not traditional city services. Therefore, the reason for having a classification. As to alternate channels of communication, the Plain Dealer can have a newsboy selling it their. Their newsboys do go up and down the street, up and down the drive­ ways, and deliver newspapers. Their drivers come to the commercial areas. They unload —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Fischer

turns.name: HENRY B. FISCHER
turns.text: Sorry. QUESTION,:. Mt* Carrier. ORAL ARGUMENT BY JAMES P. GARNER, ESOUIRE

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Justice Brennan, and may it please the Court, I do not agree with too much of what Mr. Fischer said, but I believe he did recognize that we claim, at least, and he is absolutely correct, that this case involves a potential and a very serious potential for censorship. More specifically, the City of Lakewood originally tried an outright ban on the news racks, and the District Court found that to be unconstitutional. The city abandoned that: route and enacted the ordinance —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible) the District Court was wrong about that?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: If the District Court was wrong, the --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And the city could have kept —”

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Could have appealed, yes — if I might be precise, Your Honor, we filed our complaint and a motion for summary judqment based on the illegality of the violation of our First Amendment rights with respect to the complete ban. The judge summoned us into chambers, told us what his decision was some time after the motion had been submitted, and said that he was granting the motion but was holding in abeyance for a period of six days the entry of the final order to permit the city if it chose to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's assume that the city did have the constitutional right to keep the Plain Dealer from havind any boxes on the city streets. Would that make any difference in this case?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I don't think so, Your Honor. Not to the issues before Your Honor. They do permit us there, and I think the precedents of this Court when you have this unfettered, complete discretion in city officials, not only the mayor.: but the architectural board, the precedents are clear that it is facially challenged on that ground. It is the potential 'fer censorship which results from that which creates the constitutional confrontation which we feel must be resolved in our favor. So we feel the complete ban is not before Your Honors, and we feel that the discretion in the mayor, unfettered., the architectural board, no standards nor guides whatsoever, and this is not, we suggest —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Garner, before you get all the way into the merits --

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — I had missed the fact that the city was really making an independent jurisdictional argument which they have advanced here«on the ground as I understand it that all you had to do was apply for these rental units and no doubt you could have had them.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I don't know whether we could have had them or not, Your Honor. so found.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: If the mayor found that no reasonable reason to deny us or he found no necessary reason to deny us, presumably we might have had our permit, but it is the potential for censorship here. We are talking for the moment about the City of Lakewood, but if I could make a short excursion outside the record, in the city — in the County separate municipalities. Now, whether or not Mayor Sinagra would have readily issued the permit to the Plain Dealer — by the way, he was- reelected yesterday, and he serves another term —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: (Inaudible.)

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Endorsed by the Plain Dealer, I might add, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is why you are sure you will get your boxes.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Sir? I'm sorry?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That is why you are sure you will get your boxes.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: The experience that the Plain Dealer might well anticipate from some of the other municipalities where there have been rather detailed investigations and stories in the Plain Dealer with respect to city affairs, I think there would be serious question if this unfettered discretion, and there was no right by the Plain Dealer really effectively to go anywhere. The mayor says, no, that's it. He says safety, I'm not satisfied, that's the end of it. The architectural board said it's not-pretty enough. That's the end of it. We have no place to go.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I thought there was an appeal process.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, but I think the appeals process when we are talking about First Amendment rights is insufficient. I think the — for example, this case started in 1982. Here we are almost to 1988. I think the precedents of this Court are ample to support the proposition that when 'First Amendment rights are denied, that some kind of an appellate process is sufficient to rectify it. It probably would be anywhere from two to four years to bbtain a complete administrative and judicial review in the State of Ohio through the Ohio Supreme Court, and that we submit just does not square with the notion of First Amendment rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Garner —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: That could happen — that could happen if you had precise standards and you were denied and you just claimed the mayor made a mistake, and you had to go up on appeal, where you might not hear for two-or three years.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: If we — well, if we had precise standards I don't see there would be much to be arguing about.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, whatever the standard is, somebody can misapply it..

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I think in this case news racks are capable of very precise legislation. We are not talking about something nebulous about words that might incite somebody to a riot or something. We are talking about a news rack. Everyone knows what they look like. Everyone knows how big they are. If- the mayor or the city council feels that five feet is the distance it must be away from a fire hydrant, so be it Five feet, not four, five or more.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, Mr. Garner, but suppose the regulation is no more than one news rack on any corner or whatever it might be, and then there is some dispute about which one of the competing newspapers wants that corner.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: On numbers, Your Honor, the City of Lakewood has a requirement of no more than three abreast at this time or at any one location, three abreast. At this time or at the time this commenced the Plain Dealer did not complairi about that, feeling that that is within the pne of what is reasonable in the City of Lakewood. Whether that would be true in another city, I don't know, Your Honor. It would depend on, I believe, the application of some of the tests developed by Your Honors in other cases, the Rockford school ca9«, the Grayned test, the test of compatibility. If five is compatible, then that is right. Some cities have-.-a great many more newspapers to be distributed. Coming back to your precise question, I think a limit of one would be suspect for two reasons. One, I think it is too restrictive, and secondly, it does offer perhaps even a pretextual use of —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, we can't even three do that. The record suggests —

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: which — a precise selection of which publisher can occupy that spot.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: The record suggests U.S. News and World Report gets one, New York Times another, and the Plain Dealer the third. Supposing a fourth paper comes in, and they say, well, we have already used up all the three stand slots?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Wellj they would have to move — they would have to move down the street a ways. They are not forbidden anywhere else in the city. Just, they can't attach it --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, supposing they make a map, and on the map they list all the permissible sites, and no more than three at any one of these sites, and you people all get these three, and a fourth paper comes in. I guess they are just out of luck.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I don't believe, Your Honor —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Real precise standard.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: — the map listed all the permissible sites. I believe what is in that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, I am giving you a hypothetical.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Oh.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: A hypothetical ordinance. You get all the standards you want in, and they are very, very definite, but these are the sites, no more than three at any one of these 3Q or 40 sites, they are all taken, and somebody else comes along and said, we would like a site.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: If we are talking about the traditional public forum here, which is, of course, the entire sidewalk, an entire block long, and it is difficult to imagine that three boxes would occupy the whole block. They would occupy that portion of the block, but the rest of that block would be available for other publications.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And suppose they then say, well, since we can't accommodate all four without having what we consider visual blight by too many of these, we just decided not to allow any.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I think that a complete' ban on boxes — is that Your Honor's question —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes. MR* GARNER: — anywhere in the jurisdiction? QUESTION* I am just realy not sure that isn't some — MR,T GARNER: Again, I don't believe it is in the case, Your Honor, but I think it would cleairly be unconsti­ tutional .

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What case from this Court do you cite for that conclusion?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: The exercise of First Amendment rights?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: No, no, that a flat ban on the renting of space for vending machines would come —

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: We don't agree that we are renting space. That is the city's characterization„of what is occurring here, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I am just asking what case you rely upon from this Court that space on the sidewalk must be permanently set aside for the newspaper's vending machines.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: We don't — I can't quite agree with your characterization of permanently set aside.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Stop disagreeing with my characterization and give me a citation,, if you would.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I think any of the cases, Lovell, or any of the cases, Shuttlesworth, Coxby, Louisiana, any of the cases which talk about the exercise of First Amendment rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: - I just didn't find any of those directly relevant to the giving up of space on a city sidewalk or otherwise,for a permanent device of some kind.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I don't believe that it is a permanent giving up. I said we —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is not hourly, is it?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: No, no, it is not hourly, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It isn't yearly either. MR, GARNER: Well, it could be whatever —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, if you quit —

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: — whatever is reasonably necessary, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If you didn't get enough nickels and dimes in it, you would move it, I suppose.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I think that's probably correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Up until-that time, it is going to be there.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Not if there is a legitimate or logical reason for moving it, street repairs, parade going to occupy the property, whatever reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could I ask you on the discretion-—

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: — end, take the city — is it the city architect, architectural board?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes..

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if the ordinance said, the design of these 25 by 50 inch boxes shall be the design specified by the architectural board, and the architectural board meets, and it publishes a specific design. If you want a news box, you would have to have it this way, no other way. And there is a — you wouldn't have any objection if in the ordinance it says 25 by 50 and the design is as follows colon, and it has a. drawing in it of the precise —

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: If — two or three observations, Your Honor. One, if it.is a box, if there is a box available that could meet the specifications, obviously.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, you would have to make one.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I am not sure that would be possible. You might be coming back to the —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, then you are back to whether there is a constitutional right to get on the sidewalk. What about my — what if it were perfectly feasible to make a box precisely to conform with the architectural board's specifications? Would you say that that is uncontrolled disrection?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I don't think if. it is precise and it is reasonable and it is available, I can't say that it is necessarily wholly unreasonable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But having the architecture board have so little bit of discretion even gives you a little bit more room. It is more favorable to you.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Having the architectural board with this unlimited discretion permits them to turn down any box that they desire to turn down, no matter what the publisher is, whether it is because of the box or whether it is a pretextual reason.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I would think you would rather have it this way and have the architectural board specify in detail what your box has to be. "MR. GARNER: We would be pleased and, I think, would have been pleased had we been able to arrive at a complete agreement with the City of Lakewood with respect to speci­ ficity in the ordinance from top to bottom. We failed in that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did you ever ask the architectural board what kind of a box they might approve?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: They had no idea. They had never considered the matter. The testimony in the record says they —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, did you ask them?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: They were asked at the trial, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you never showed them a box?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: No, no. We are — as I believe we mentioned earlier, Your Honor, we feel we have the opportunity or the right to test this uncontrolled discretion facially.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Garner, I.notice that the Court of Appeals seems to rely very heavily on Staub and City of Baxley. Was there an appellate process in that case? MR. GARNER? I don’t recall, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Garner, can I ask you, supposing we were to affirm, I may have the facts a little wrong, but the Court of Appeals enjoined the enforcement of the ordinance in total, didn't they?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Not entirely. It separated the ban in areas zoned residential, and struck down the rest of it really three — they found three flaws, three constitutional flaws, and they struck down all of the rest except for the residential ban because it was not separable.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Did they strike down the portion of the ordinance that authorizes the city to rent the property for this purpose?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: They struck that down, yes.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So if we just were to affirm, there would in effect be no ordinance pertaining to this sbject on the books.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: They have enacted a new ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Oh, they have a new ordinance.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes, sir. Yes, Justice Stevens. In 1987, earlier this year, I believe, they enacted the new one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: \Qhat happens through this lawsuit if we get another ordinance in place? QUESTION’: I thought the new ordinance was just temoo' rary, pending the resolution of • this ‘lawsuitIs that-not right?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: T don't believe so, ’Your‘Honor. What, it says is that it suspends the operation of the mayor's discretion and the architectural board's discretion. ^hose are not part of the new ordinance. The unlimited discretion.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Suspends? You mean until when? MR, GARNER: Until some 60 days after this Court reaches its decision, or until •—

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it is just temporary, pending the resolution of the case?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, those particular points are, yes. The rest of it is all in position.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, Mr. Garner, was the idea that if this Court affirmed, then after 60 days would not the interim ordinance continue in effect?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes. That is my understanding, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Could that be interpreted as a threat?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: As a threat?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I don't know what would happen, depending* on what this Court does, obviously. I think they might very well go back to where they started, the complete ban.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they are trying to influence this.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yea. Well, I don't know, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For what other reason would they pass it?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Would they pass it? Well —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: For what other reason?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because the ^lain Dealer wanted their boxes on the street.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But I am trying to understand, the substitute ordinance is now in effect pending the outcome of this case?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: ^he substitute ordinance is substantially in toto in effect. The portions that are not in

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Without the objectionable feature?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Excuse me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Without the objectionable features.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes, that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: : Why not?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Because if —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Because, you know, time is of the essence in these First Amendment issues, I understand. You can't wait for an appeal.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: You had better get those boxes out there.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, Your Honor —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Perhaps you don't have enouqh confidence in your case here. think this is a very important case, and from the Plain

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: In Lakewood?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Not to the best of my knowledge, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But no one has taken advantage of this * interim ordinance?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: As far as I know, that's correct. QUESTION! Because if there hadn't been this interim ordinance, there wouldn't have been any ordinance in effect at all after the Court of Appeals' judgment.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: ^hat's correct. That is correct. Anybody would have been free to —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And then if you had wanted to get on the street, you would have a little problem getting your boxes on the street without an ordinance.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Without an ordinance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Or would you just say we are going to put them up^ because we have the constitutional right to be there?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I think that's — I can't soeak -- yes, it is true, Your Honor. In a number of cities there are no ordinances whatsoever dealing with news racks.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes, but they don't object to the newspapers being there.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Right, they acquiesce in it, correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: They acquiesce in the unfettered discretion of the mayor, I presume. 'HR. GARNER* Well, if it is unfettered, it is unexercised as well.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, he exercises it by not objecting.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, they are out in front of this building. You can buy the — whatever you want out there, except the Plain Dealer.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I think, Your Honor, there is probably more call for different publications in the City of Washington certainly than the City of Lakewood, Ohio. A great many more publishers want their newspaper here in the nation's capital than they do in the City of Cleveland or its suburban areas.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Of course, that doesn't mean it wasn't litigated. It was, a few years ago.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes. With respect to the insurance I would say — make a couple of observation. One has two parts to it. One is the indemnity which, as I read it, is so broad that the Plain Dealer or the press, small "o", is called upon to indemnify the city for things that may even be the city's fault if there is a news rack even collaterally involved one way or another, and I think that kind of provision, without respect to the First Amendment, would be abhorrent. More importantly, the insurance, from the questions of the Court, I believe they were all directed toward the fact that the requirement is solely for insurance to be provided by the newspaper publishers who use these news racks, the other orivatelV owned utilities. Nobody else is called upon for the same thing. We think that quite clearly singles out the press in an unconstitutional —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, the city takes the position, I guess, that it is renting the space for the vending devices and that it would charge — require the furnishing of insurance for anyone who rented space from the city.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes. May I state my view of what I think the relationship is of the Plain Dealer or a publisher with its rack on the street and the city? We don't view it as a possessory interest of any sort, nor do we view it as a property right. The word "rental" connotes to me some sort of a passage of an interest in real property, paying money for a right to do something. It is assignable, it is this, it is that. What I conceive or perceive we have is a First Amendment right, the same as it would if we had a news hawker out there. A news hawker can stand there», We have a First —-

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if we think that it is different,

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Yes. Oh* there is no question about

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Twenty-four hours a day. We think that it far better serves the public that may desire ±o buy newspapers. It serves them 24 hours a day.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But you assert that it is not possible

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What if they say that for every situation in which they are granting a permit to physically occupy city property they are going to require that type of insurance?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Do they do the same with the electric utility?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Too late, they say. Water over the dam. Before the city’ ever thought of such things, they had given an easement already.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I think the city council — certainly that could be rectified, Justice Scalia.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Not if the easement has been granted already, no.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Well, I think that — I don't know the answer to that, Your Honor. It depends a lot on statutory —- state statutes which establish the relationship of some of these municipalities and the utilities, and I am not an authority on that subject, but if there were a demonstrated need that there were safety hazards involved with the tele­ phone polls, I am sure one way or another a city would be successful in obtaining insurance coverage to protect it. I might add that., the record is barren here to show that there has ever been a safety factor with respect to news racks. It just doesn't exist.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Let's assume, carrying Justice O'Connor's question further, that we do think that this is somehow a specific, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I didn't ask whether it could be very specific. I asked a simple question, must every provision of the lease contract be in the city ordinance, including the price, and the mayor would not have the ability to alter the price from year to year. Would that be tbo much discretion? I mean, I am trying to tftihk of how you could set up a sensible system. It doesn't seem to me the city is being vindictive or coming down on the press which endorses their mayor or anything like that. It seems to me they are trying to do a reasonable thing, and I'd like to know, what do you suggest they could have done differently? For instance, that provision — such are the terms and conditions deemed necessary and reasonable by the mayor. Well, I can think of a million things that could come up, why in one particular location you have to add an additional condition besides the ones you normally think of, this location is near a school or who knows what? I think it seems to me quite reasonable to put in that residual clause. Now, you don't like it. What would up subsequently could be very well handled at the next regular or special meeting .of the council.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: May I ask you a question on another subject? Are there any ordinances either in Cuyahoga County that pertain to the retail sale of newspapers, magazines, and the like on public property? I know in a lot of cities there are public newsstands that are operated by human beings rather than these inanimate objects. Are they generally the subject of ordinance?

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: So far as I know, there are no public newsstands on public property in Cuyahoga County.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In that area. I see.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: I know of none. Unlike Washington. I don't recall ever having seen one.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Bus stations. The railroad station.

turns.name: JAMES P. GARNER
turns.text: Oh, yes, the stations. DOCKET NUMBER: 86-1042 CASE TITLE: City of Lakewood vs. Plain Dealer Publiching Co HEARING DATE: November 4, 1987 LOCATION: Washington, D.C. Date: November 12, 1987 1220 L Street, N.W.
